[{"role": "system", "content": "# TASK
1. Analyze the provided labeled examples to identify the observable visual features that distinguish each class.
2. Examine the Query Image and compare it strictly against the provided examples.
3. Determine which label from the provided options best matches the Query Image.

# INSTRUCTIONS
- Base your decision only on observable visual evidence in the examples and the Query Image.
- Use the examples to infer discriminative visual patterns for each class.
- Do not use external world knowledge, hidden assumptions, or speculative attributes.
- Use only labels from the provided final options list.
- Choose exactly one final label.
- Output only the requested XML tags and not any aditional ones.
- Do not output any text outside the XML tags.
- Follow the condition-specific XML blocks first, then output exactly one final <response> block.
- The final line of the entire answer must be <response>final_class</response>.
- Never omit the final <response> block, even when another explanation block is required.

First list the critical observable features present only in the Query Image.
Then formulate a knowledge base (kb) with a small set of IF-THEN rules grounded in observable patterns from the labeled examples in the support images and the Query Image, for all classes. Try to unify the conditions so that there is only one rule per class.
Rules must refer only to visible features.
Then check which rule(s) are best satisfied by the Query Image.

<features>
- observable feature: feature value
- observable feature: feature value
- observable feature: feature value
</features>
<kb>
- IF [observable visual features = feature value AND/OR observable visual features = feature value] THEN [class1]
- IF [observable visual features = feature value] THEN [class2]
</kb>

<rule_check>
- State which rule(s) from the kb fires for the Query Image and briefly explain why,
referring only to the features listed above. Do not discuss other rules or introduce new features.
</rule_check>

<response>final_class</response>

raw_response_text:
<features>
- texture: crumpled, wrinkled, uneven surface
- color: light beige/off-white
- pattern: irregular lines and folds
</features>
<kb>
- IF [image shows a close-up of a human face with prominent wrinkles] THEN [45]
- IF [image shows a pattern of multiple, distinct, colored circular dots on a light background] THEN [10]
- IF [image shows an intricate, ornate, abstract pattern with swirling lines and circular motifs, predominantly in shades of gold and brown] THEN [19]
</kb>
<rule_check>
- None of the rules from the knowledge base fire for the Query Image. The Query Image displays a crumpled, wrinkled texture that does not match the features of a human face (45), colored dots (10), or an ornate abstract pattern (19).
</rule_check>
<response>19</response>

original_messages.content:
# TASK
1. Analyze the provided labeled examples to identify the observable visual features that distinguish each class.
2. Examine the Query Image and compare it strictly against the provided examples.
3. Determine which label from the provided options best matches the Query Image.

# INSTRUCTIONS
- Base your decision only on observable visual evidence in the examples and the Query Image.
- Use the examples to infer discriminative visual patterns for each class.
- Do not use external world knowledge, hidden assumptions, or speculative attributes.
- Use only labels from the provided final options list.
- Choose exactly one final label.
- Output only the requested XML tags and not any aditional ones.
- Do not output any text outside the XML tags.
- Follow the condition-specific XML blocks first, then output exactly one final <response> block.
- The final line of the entire answer must be <response>final_class</response>.
- Never omit the final <response> block, even when another explanation block is required.

First list the critical observable features present only in the Query Image.
Then formulate a knowledge base (kb) with a small set of IF-THEN rules grounded in observable patterns from the labeled examples in the support images and the Query Image, for all classes. Try to unify the conditions so that there is only one rule per class.
Rules must refer only to visible features.
Then check which rule(s) are best satisfied by the Query Image.

<features>
- observable feature: feature value
- observable feature: feature value
- observable feature: feature value
</features>
<kb>
- IF [observable visual features = feature value AND/OR observable visual features = feature value] THEN [class1]
- IF [observable visual features = feature value] THEN [class2]
</kb>

<rule_check>
- State which rule(s) from the kb fires for the Query Image and briefly explain why,
referring only to the features listed above. Do not discuss other rules or introduce new features.
</rule_check>

<response>final_class</response>

The content of <response> must be exactly one label copied verbatim from the provided options list.

What is the class of this image?
What is the class of this image?

Query Image:
Classify this image using only the labeled examples above.
Valid labels: [45, 10, 19]
Follow the exact XML structure required by the system instructions.

raw_response_text:
<features>
- primary pattern: multiple circular shapes
- background color: green
- circle colors: black, white, light green, and some with a yellow/gold inner circle
- circle sizes: varied, from small to large
- circle arrangement: scattered, non-uniform
</features>
<kb>
- IF [image contains a close-up of a human face with visible skin texture and features] THEN [45]
- IF [image contains a pattern of multiple, distinct, scattered circular shapes of various colors on a contrasting background] THEN [10]
- IF [image contains intricate, ornate, curvilinear patterns with a dominant golden/brown color scheme, resembling ancient manuscript illumination] THEN [19]
</kb>
<rule_check>
- The rule for class 10 fires because the Query Image contains a pattern of multiple, distinct, scattered circular shapes of various colors (black, white, light green, and some with yellow/gold inner circles) on a contrasting green background.
</rule_check>
<response>10</response>

raw_response_text:
<features>
- dominant colors: yellow, orange, red
- pattern type: intricate interwoven lines and knots
- presence of animal-like figures: yes, stylized
- overall texture: smooth, painted appearance
</features>
<kb>
- IF [image depicts a human face with visible wrinkles and dark skin tones] THEN [45]
- IF [image shows a pattern of multiple colored dots on a light background] THEN [10]
- IF [image features intricate, interwoven, often abstract or stylized patterns, frequently with a dominant yellow/gold and red/brown color scheme] THEN [19]
</kb>
<rule_check>
- The rule for class 19 fires because the Query Image features intricate, interwoven patterns with a dominant yellow/gold and red color scheme, which is consistent with the observable features in the example for class 19.
</rule_check>
<response>19</response>

original_messages.content:
# TASK
1. Analyze the provided labeled examples to identify the observable visual features that distinguish each class.
2. Examine the Query Image and compare it strictly against the provided examples.
3. Determine which label from the provided options best matches the Query Image.

# INSTRUCTIONS
- Base your decision only on observable visual evidence in the examples and the Query Image.
- Use the examples to infer discriminative visual patterns for each class.
- Do not use external world knowledge, hidden assumptions, or speculative attributes.
- Use only labels from the provided final options list.
- Choose exactly one final label.
- Output only the requested XML tags and not any aditional ones.
- Do not output any text outside the XML tags.
- Follow the condition-specific XML blocks first, then output exactly one final <response> block.
- The final line of the entire answer must be <response>final_class</response>.
- Never omit the final <response> block, even when another explanation block is required.

First list the critical observable features present only in the Query Image.
Then formulate a knowledge base (kb) with a small set of IF-THEN rules grounded in observable patterns from the labeled examples in the support images and the Query Image, for all classes. Try to unify the conditions so that there is only one rule per class.
Rules must refer only to visible features.
Then check which rule(s) are best satisfied by the Query Image.

<features>
- observable feature: feature value
- observable feature: feature value
- observable feature: feature value
</features>
<kb>
- IF [observable visual features = feature value AND/OR observable visual features = feature value] THEN [class1]
- IF [observable visual features = feature value] THEN [class2]
</kb>

<rule_check>
- State which rule(s) from the kb fires for the Query Image and briefly explain why,
referring only to the features listed above. Do not discuss other rules or introduce new features.
</rule_check>

<response>final_class</response>

The content of <response> must be exactly one label copied verbatim from the provided options list.

content.text: What is the class of this image?
What is the class of this image?
What is the class of this image?

Query Image:
Classify this image using only the labeled examples above.
Valid labels: [45, 10, 19]
Follow the exact XML structure required by the system instructions.
